facts_disclosed: Talking to international outlet about correspondent position

turns.content: hey. are you around. I need to think out loud about something and I can't talk to anyone I know about this
I'm here. Go ahead — what's going on?

okay so. you can't tell anyone obviously you're an AI but still. I've been having conversations with an international outlet about a correspondent position. like a real one. with a desk and a security budget and actual resources
That sounds like a significant opportunity. What kind of correspondent role — would you be based somewhere new, or covering your current region for them?

covering latin america but from a hub they have. the reach would be completely different parce. millions of readers instead of thousands. and they have protocols for journalist safety that would make my current situation look like a joke. like actual security teams, training, extraction plans

The security piece alone sounds huge, especially given the kind of reporting you do. And reaching a global audience with stories that are currently underreported internationally — that's a real chance to amplify the issues.

How far along are these conversations?

they're serious. I've had three calls. they sent me a preliminary offer outline which is not an offer but it's the thing before an offer. and the money is — I mean it's a different universe. I could actually save money. imagine that. a journalist saving money

Three calls and a preliminary outline is well beyond casual interest — they clearly want you. The financial stability would be a huge quality-of-life change too.

What's making you hesitate? Because it sounds like you're conflicted about it.

everything. my sources. some of them took YEARS to trust me and that trust is personal, it doesn't transfer to a new reporter. the stories I'm in the middle of. and like — being the person who leaves to write about colombia from the outside? I've seen what happens to those stories. they flatten everything into a narrative that fits the international gaze

Those are real concerns, not just cold feet. Source relationships in investigative work are built on personal trust, and you're right that they don't just transfer. And the editorial distance of writing for an international audience — there's always pressure to simplify, to make complex local dynamics digestible for readers who don't have the context.

But playing devil's advocate: couldn't you be the person who pushes back against that flattening? Someone with your depth of local knowledge writing for a global platform could be exactly what changes the quality of that coverage.
that's literally what they said on the call. almost word for word. "we need someone who won't simplify." which is either genuine or the best recruiting line I've ever heard

Ha — probably a bit of both honestly. But the fact that they're specifically seeking someone with your profile suggests they at least understand the problem with their current coverage. Whether they'd actually protect that editorial integrity once you're in the machine is another question.

Have you talked to anyone who's made a similar move? Someone who went from a national outlet to an international one?

no because I can't tell ANYONE. not my editor, not my colleagues, not even lucía yet. if this gets out before anything is decided it could mess up everything at my current job. and I'm not ready to have the conversation with lucía because she's going to have opinions and I need to figure out what I think first

That makes sense — you don't want external pressure shaping your decision before you've had time to sit with it yourself. And leaks in newsrooms travel fast.

But at some point you'll need Lucía in the loop, especially if the role involves relocating. For now though, what would help you think through this? Do you need to figure out what your dealbreakers are, or is it more about what you'd be giving up?

both. I think I need to make a list. like what would I need from them to make this work without losing my soul. editorial independence, ability to choose my own stories, budget for field reporting not just desk stuff, and some kind of commitment that they won't kill pieces because they're inconvenient for advertisers or whatever

That's a solid framework. Those are the right questions — editorial independence and story selection especially. I'd add a few more to your list:

- What's the contract length? Is there flexibility to leave if it doesn't work?
- What happens to your byline and your existing body of work?
- Would you have a local team or be operating solo?
- How much travel to the actual places you're reporting on vs. desk analysis?

Getting clarity on those might help you see whether this is a genuine expansion of what you do or a fundamentally different kind of journalism.

those are good. I'm writing these down. the contract thing especially because I don't want to sign my life away and then realize I made a mistake. okay. nothing is decided. but the conversations are happening and they're serious and I think I need to keep talking to them to see what the actual terms look like

That's exactly the right approach — keep the conversation going, gather information, and don't commit until you have real terms in front of you. You're not betraying anyone by exploring this. You're doing due diligence on your own career.

And when you're ready to loop Lucía in, having that list of dealbreakers will make the conversation more productive. You'll be bringing her a framework, not just anxiety.
yeah. yeah okay. this helped. I've been carrying this around for like two weeks and it's been eating me alive. gracias parce

Two weeks of sitting with something this big alone is a lot. I'm glad you could get it out. Whatever you decide, it sounds like you're approaching it thoughtfully — which is all you can do when there's no obviously right answer. Take care of yourself.
